title: Autoencoders as a Simple Generative Idea
concept: An autoencoder compresses an input into a compact latent code and reconstructs it (recall Unit 172's reconstruction-based anomaly detection). That hints at generative capability, but a standard autoencoder isn't actually trained to produce convincing brand-new samples the way a true generative model is.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In generative modeling, what does it mean for a model to "sample from a distribution" it learned?

options.text: It picks a fresh point from the learned pattern of the training data to produce a new, plausible example
It randomly selects and copies one exact file from the training set
It measures how far apart two training images are in pixels

exercises.explain: Yes! Sampling means drawing a new point from the learned pattern, producing a genuinely new example.

cards.title: A network you've already met
cards.body: You've already worked with a network that squeezes an image down into a tiny code and rebuilds it from scratch — Unit 172's reconstruction-based anomaly detector, where a large reconstruction error was the anomaly signal. Could that same squeeze-and-rebuild trick be used to dream up brand-new images?

cards.title: What is an autoencoder?
cards.body: An autoencoder is a neural network with two halves: an encoder that compresses an input into a compact internal representation (a latent code), and a decoder that reconstructs the original input from that code. Recall Unit 172: a normal-trained autoencoder reconstructs normal inputs well, and struggles on anomalies.

cards.title: A photocopier with a sticky note
cards.body: An autoencoder is like a photocopier forced to shrink a full page down to a single sticky note of shorthand notes, then redraw the entire page from just those notes. It gets remarkably good at reproducing pages similar to ones it has seen — but it was never trained to draw a brand-new page purely from imagination.

cards.title: Feeding it noise instead of a real code
cards.body: Feed a trained face autoencoder a real face photo, and its decoder reconstructs a close copy — no surprise there. But feed the decoder pure random noise in place of a real encoded photo, and it usually produces a blurry, unconvincing mess, not a crisp new face.

cards.title: Reconstructing isn't the same as generating
cards.body: Since autoencoders can rebuild images, it's tempting to assume they can generate any new image on demand. In reality, a standard autoencoder is trained only to minimize reconstruction error on real inputs — its latent space isn't organized to make random sampling produce realistic results. That gap is exactly what purpose-built generative models are designed to close.

cards.title: Autoencoder vs. a true generative model
cards.body: Same squeeze-and-rebuild shape, very different training goals:

left.text:
Trained to reconstruct real input
Great at rebuilding what it has seen
Weak at inventing from random input

right.text:
Trained to produce convincing output
from random input, no real input needed
Purpose-built for novel generation

exercises.prompt: What are the two halves of an autoencoder?

options.text: An encoder that compresses the input, and a decoder that reconstructs it
A generator that creates fakes, and a discriminator that judges them
A classifier head and a detector head
A training loop and a validation loop

exercises.explain: Correct! Encoder compresses to a latent code, decoder reconstructs from that code.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A standard autoencoder is explicitly trained to produce realistic new images from random noise.
exercises.explain: False. It's trained to minimize reconstruction error on real inputs — feeding it random noise usually yields a blurry, unconvincing result.

exercises.sentence: An autoencoder's decoder reconstructs an image from a compact ___ representation created by the encoder.
exercises.explain: The compressed internal representation an encoder produces is called a latent code.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes what an autoencoder was actually trained to do?

options.text: Minimize the difference between a real input and its reconstruction after compression
Maximize how convincing its output looks when starting from pure random noise
Compete against a second network that tries to catch its fakes

exercises.explain: Correct! Reconstruction accuracy on real inputs is the autoencoder's actual training goal.

exercises.prompt: An engineer feeds pure random static into a pretrained face autoencoder's decoder, hoping it generates a brand-new realistic face. What most likely happens, and why?

options.text: It likely produces a blurry, unrealistic image, because the autoencoder was only trained to reconstruct real encoded inputs, not to produce convincing output from arbitrary random codes
It produces a perfectly realistic new face, because autoencoders are fully general-purpose generators
It crashes, because autoencoders cannot accept any input besides real photos
It produces an exact copy of a training photo, since autoencoders always memorize their inputs

exercises.explain: Exactly! Reconstruction skill on real inputs doesn't transfer to convincing generation from random codes. Next: GANs close that gap with a completely different training idea — competition.
